column3: Organization size
column11: The organization is a small team with fewer than 50 employees, enabling fast decision-making but limited formal structures around AI adoption.

column3: Industry / sector
column11: The organization operates in the technology sector, with a strong focus on software development and engineering productivity.

column3: Primary motivation for AI adoption
column11: AI is primarily adopted to improve developer productivity, especially by accelerating coding, debugging, and documentation tasks rather than driving new AI-based products or revenue streams.

column3: Current AI adoption stage (self-assessment)
column11: AI usage is largely experimental and tool-driven, focused on individual productivity rather than formalized, organization-wide AI systems or workflows.

column3: Urgency for AI impact
column11: While AI impact is not mission-critical in the immediate term, there is a clear expectation that AI-driven efficiency gains will be important within the next 6–12 months.

column3: Is there a clearly defined owner or governing body for AI decisions?
column11: AI-related decisions are handled informally by engineering leadership, without a formally designated AI owner or governance body.

column3: Does your organization have a formal AI ethics or responsible-AI policy?
column11: There is currently no formal AI ethics or responsible-AI policy; considerations are handled on an ad-hoc basis when questions arise.

column3: How are AI-related risks (bias, security, regulatory) assessed?
column11: Potential AI risks such as security or misuse are discussed informally within the engineering team but are not assessed through a structured framework.

column3: How consistently are AI governance standards applied across teams?
column11: Since governance standards are informal, application varies by team and individual practices rather than being consistently enforced.

column3: How are regulatory and compliance requirements handled for AI?
column11: Regulatory and compliance considerations related to AI are addressed reactively if and when they become relevant.

column3: How mature is AI auditability and documentation?
column11: Documentation related to AI usage is minimal and typically limited to general engineering notes rather than formal audit-ready records.

column3: How standardized are data definitions and business semantics?
column11: Data definitions are aligned informally through team communication, without a shared or formally maintained data glossary.

column3: How accessible are high-value datasets for AI use?
column11: Access to datasets is handled manually and on a case-by-case basis, reflecting limited AI-driven data usage.

column3: How are third-party data sources evaluated?
column11: The organization does not currently use or formally evaluate third-party data sources for AI purposes.

column3: How well is unstructured data supported?
column11: Unstructured data is handled manually when needed, without centralized pipelines or AI-ready preprocessing.

column3: How mature is enterprise data governance?
column11: Data ownership exists informally within teams, but there are no formal governance roles or enterprise-wide data policies.

column3: How automated are data pipelines?
column11: Data handling relies on simple scripts or manual processes rather than fully automated, monitored pipelines.

column3: How suitable is infrastructure for AI at scale?
column11: The organization uses basic cloud infrastructure sufficient for development and experimentation, but not optimized for large-scale AI workloads.

column3: How portable are AI workloads?
column11: AI-related workflows depend heavily on specific tools and environments, making portability limited without rework.

column3: How does architecture support experimentation?
column11: Development and testing environments are separated, enabling limited experimentation without impacting production systems.

column3: How modular are AI platform components?
column11: AI tools are integrated into existing development workflows but are not designed as modular, easily replaceable components.

column3: How future-ready is the AI architecture?
column11: The current architecture provides some flexibility for future AI adoption, though significant enhancements would be required to support advanced use cases.

column3: How standardized are AI tools across teams?
column11: Teams have converged on a small set of preferred AI coding tools, creating light standardization without formal enforcement.

column2: Section D: AI Use Case & Portfolio Management
column3: How are AI use cases identified and prioritized?
column11: AI use cases emerge organically through team discussions rather than structured prioritization processes.

column2: Section D: AI Use Case & Portfolio Management
column3: How mature are AI pilot projects?
column11: AI usage takes the form of ad-hoc pilots focused on productivity rather than formally scoped pilot programs.

column2: Section D: AI Use Case & Portfolio Management
column3: How often do pilots reach production?
column11: Some AI-assisted workflows become part of daily development practices, but many remain optional or experimental.

column2: Section D: AI Use Case & Portfolio Management
column3: How are AI outcomes tied to KPIs?
column11: AI benefits are discussed qualitatively, such as perceived productivity gains, rather than being tied to formal KPIs.

column2: Section D: AI Use Case & Portfolio Management
column3: How does the organization decide not to use AI?
column11: Decisions not to use AI are typically based on engineering judgment and perceived complexity rather than formal evaluation criteria.

column2: Section E: Model Development, Deployment & Monitoring
column3: How structured is AI model deployment?
column11: The organization does not deploy proprietary AI models; AI usage relies on external tools integrated manually into workflows.

column2: Section E: Model Development, Deployment & Monitoring
column3: How are deployed models monitored?
column11: There is no direct monitoring of AI models, as model performance is managed by third-party providers.

column2: Section E: Model Development, Deployment & Monitoring
column3: How is model explainability handled?
column11: Explainability is addressed informally through user understanding of AI outputs rather than formal tools or documentation.

column2: Section E: Model Development, Deployment & Monitoring
column3: How are bias and fairness monitored post-deployment?
column11: Bias and fairness are not formally monitored due to the limited scope of AI usage and reliance on third-party models.

column2: Section E: Model Development, Deployment & Monitoring
column3: How are AI failures handled operationally?
column11: AI-related issues are handled on a case-by-case basis, typically by disabling or bypassing AI tools when problems arise.

column2: Section E: Model Development, Deployment & Monitoring
column3: How mature is the AI incident learning loop?
column11: Lessons learned from AI-related issues are shared informally within the team without structured post-incident reviews.

column3: How are human-in-the-loop controls used?
column11: Developers manually review and validate AI-generated code before use, ensuring human oversight in all cases.

column3: How adaptive is human oversight?
column11: Human oversight is applied informally based on developer judgment rather than defined risk tiers or automation rules.

column3: How integrated are AI systems into business processes?
column11: AI tools are integrated into individual developer workflows but not deeply embedded into broader business processes.

column3: To what extent does AI automate end-to-end processes?
column11: AI supports task-level automation such as code generation but does not automate end-to-end workflows.

column3: How well do leaders understand AI capabilities and limits?
column11: Leadership has a high-level understanding of AI capabilities and limitations but does not deeply evaluate technical trade-offs.

column3: How are AI roles organized?
column11: There are no dedicated AI roles; AI usage is embedded informally within existing engineering responsibilities.

column3: How mature is the AI skills inventory?
column11: AI-related skills are understood informally based on individual experience rather than a documented inventory.

column3: How strong are AI training programs?
column11: AI learning occurs through self-study and ad-hoc sharing rather than structured training programs.

column3: How resilient is the organization to AI talent attrition?
column11: Some knowledge is shared across the team, but expertise still depends on individual contributors.

column3: How clearly are AI priorities communicated?
column11: AI priorities are communicated at the leadership level, primarily around productivity improvements, but not formalized into enterprise-wide objectives.

column3: How central is AI to long-term competitive strategy?
column11: AI is viewed as an important experimental capability for improving efficiency rather than a core long-term competitive differentiator.
